stem:
A 68-year-old postmenopausal woman attends your general practice clinic with a recent low-trauma right wrist fracture sustained from a slip in her garden. She says 'I fell on my outstretched hand from standing height onto grass'. She has a background of well-controlled hypertension and a long history of cigarette smoking (now ceased 8 years). She had her menopause at age 51 and has not been on hormone therapy.

On examination she is well, with healed wrist plaster removed last week. She is 162 cm tall (down 4 cm from her self-reported peak height of 166 cm). She has no kyphosis on visual inspection. There is no thoracic spine tenderness. Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry done after the fracture shows lumbar spine T-score -2.6 and femoral neck T-score -2.8. Bloods show 25-hydroxyvitamin D 58 nmol/L (over 50 sufficient), corrected calcium 2.34 mmol/L (2.10-2.60), phosphate 1.0 mmol/L (0.8-1.5), creatinine 78 micromol/L (45-90), eGFR 70 mL/min/1.73 m squared. Thyroid function and parathyroid hormone are normal.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72, BP 134/82 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Healed right wrist fracture, height 162 cm (peak 166), no spine tenderness, no visible kyphosis
data_table.Investigations: DEXA: lumbar T-score -2.6, femoral neck T-score -2.8; 25-OH vitamin D 58 nmol/L (>50); corrected calcium 2.34 mmol/L (2.10-2.60); phosphate normal; PTH and thyroid function normal; eGFR 70 mL/min/1.73 m^2

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue calcium and vitamin D supplementation alone for 12 months and repeat DEXA before any further treatment
options.rationale: A minimal-trauma fracture in a woman over 50 with T-score under -1.5 is established osteoporosis under the AU MBS criteria; pharmacological treatment is indicated now, not deferred. (Source: Royal Australian College of General Practitioners Osteoporosis)
source_refs: Royal Australian College of General Practitioners Osteoporosis

options.text: Commence raloxifene 60 mg orally daily as the first-line pharmacological therapy for postmenopausal osteoporosis
options.rationale: Raloxifene is a SERM reserved for selected patients with vertebral fracture risk and no peripheral fracture concern; it does not reduce non-vertebral or hip fractures and is not first-line. (Source: AMH; Royal Australian College of General Practitioners Osteoporosis)
source_refs: Royal Australian College of General Practitioners Osteoporosis

options.text: Commence denosumab 60 mg subcutaneous every 6 months as the first-line therapy with twice-daily calcium-rich diet
options.rationale: Denosumab is an alternative when bisphosphonates are contraindicated or not tolerated, or with eGFR under 30 mL/min/1.73 m squared; it is not the AU first-line. Stopping denosumab abruptly causes rapid rebound bone loss, so the agent is best reserved for patients who can adhere indefinitely. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Refer to endocrinology for teriparatide as first-line anabolic therapy in this older postmenopausal woman
options.rationale: Teriparatide (recombinant PTH 1-34) is anabolic and reserved for patients with severe osteoporosis (T-score under -3 with multiple fractures or where antiresorptive therapy has failed); it is not first-line. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Commence oral alendronate 70 mg once weekly with calcium and vitamin D and address modifiable risk factors
options.rationale: A minimal-trauma fracture and T-score under -2.5 in a postmenopausal woman meets PBS criteria for an oral bisphosphonate. Alendronate 70 mg orally once weekly is first-line. Adequate dietary calcium (1300 mg daily) and vitamin D (with supplementation if level under 50 nmol/L), smoking abstinence, weight-bearing exercise, and fall-risk modification are co-prescribed. Bone health is reviewed at 12 months and treatment duration reviewed at 5 years. (Source: Royal Australian College of General Practitioners Osteoporosis; AMH)
source_refs: Royal Australian College of General Practitioners Osteoporosis

explanation.summary: Postmenopausal osteoporosis is diagnosed by DEXA T-score under -2.5 OR by a minimal-trauma fracture regardless of T-score in a woman over 50 (or man over 50). Australian PBS-funded therapy for women aged 70 or over uses T-score under -2.5 OR T-score under -1.5 with minimal-trauma fracture. First-line pharmacotherapy is an oral bisphosphonate (alendronate 70 mg weekly, risedronate 35 mg weekly, or zoledronic acid 5 mg IV annually). Denosumab is second-line or first-line when bisphosphonates contraindicated. Anabolic agents (teriparatide, romosozumab) are reserved for severe disease. Calcium 1300 mg daily, vitamin D supplementation, weight-bearing exercise, fall-risk modification, and smoking abstinence are universally recommended.
key_points: Diagnosis: T-score under -2.5 OR minimal-trauma fracture over 50
First-line: oral bisphosphonate (alendronate or risedronate) or IV zoledronic acid
Denosumab second-line; severe rebound on cessation
Anabolics (teriparatide, romosozumab) for severe disease only
explanation.pearls: Zoledronic acid 5 mg IV once yearly is an alternative to weekly oral bisphosphonate when oesophageal pathology, swallow difficulty, or adherence is the issue; pre-treat with paracetamol to reduce the post-infusion flu-like reaction.

sources.label: Royal Australian College of General Practitioners Osteoporosis

stem:
You are the medical registrar in the resuscitation bay. A 24-year-old motorcyclist has been brought in after a high-speed collision with a parked vehicle. The paramedics report 'he was found unconscious at the scene, with a deformed pelvis and obvious open right tibial fracture, and is bleeding heavily from the leg'. A pelvic binder has been applied and tranexamic acid 1 g intravenously was given on the way.

On arrival he is now responsive only to painful stimuli (GCS 8). His airway is patent with a Yankauer to manage blood. He is tachypnoeic and shocked. There has been an obvious large external blood loss from the right thigh and a tense haematoma over the pelvis with pelvic tenderness. The chest is clear with bilateral air entry; FAST shows no free fluid in the abdomen and no pericardial effusion. Right tibial bone is visible at the wound. He has had 1.5 L of warmed crystalloid and his blood pressure remains 78/40 mmHg. Bedside haemoglobin 92 g/L from arterial sampling; venous lactate 5.4 mmol/L (under 2.0).

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 138 regular, BP 78/40 mmHg, RR 30, SpO2 96% on 15 L/min via non-rebreather, temperature 35.4 °C, GCS 8
data_table.Examination: Tense pelvic haematoma, open right tibial fracture, ongoing bleeding from leg wound, no abdominal free fluid on FAST, no pneumothorax
data_table.Investigations: Bedside Hb 92 g/L, lactate 5.4 mmol/L; coags pending; trauma series imaging being arranged
data_table.Medications: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV in pre-hospital; warmed crystalloid 1.5 L now in

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue warmed crystalloid resuscitation to a systolic blood pressure of 110 mmHg before considering blood products
options.rationale: Crystalloid above 1-2 L worsens trauma-induced coagulopathy by dilution and cooling; uncrossmatched red cells and balanced product ratios are the next step in major haemorrhage. (Source: NBA Patient Blood Management; National Blood Authority Massive Transfusion Module)
source_refs: National Blood Authority Massive Transfusion Module

options.text: Transfuse 4 units of packed red cells alone and reassess before requesting any plasma or platelets
options.rationale: Major haemorrhage protocols use balanced product ratios (close to 1:1:1 red cells:plasma:platelets) to reduce trauma-induced coagulopathy mortality; red cells alone exacerbate dilutional coagulopathy. (Source: NBA Patient Blood Management)

options.text: Activate the massive transfusion protocol, transfuse a 1:1:1 ratio of red cells, FFP and platelets, and call for trauma team review
options.rationale: The shock index (HR over SBP) above 1, ongoing haemorrhage with pelvic and lower limb sources, and lactate over 5 mmol/L are indications for massive transfusion protocol activation. Balanced product ratios (close to 1:1:1) reduce mortality compared to red cells alone. The pelvic binder is already applied; permissive hypotension (target SBP around 90 mmHg until haemorrhage controlled) and damage control surgery follow. Calcium gluconate is given after multiple units to prevent citrate-induced hypocalcaemia. (Source: NBA Patient Blood Management; National Blood Authority Massive Transfusion Module)
source_refs: National Blood Authority Massive Transfusion Module

options.text: Hold transfusion until a formal coagulation screen and fibrinogen result returns to guide product selection
options.rationale: Major haemorrhage requires empirical balanced product transfusion immediately; awaiting laboratory results delays correction and increases mortality. Targeted product replacement is added later guided by laboratory or viscoelastic testing. (Source: NBA Patient Blood Management)

options.text: Administer prothrombinex 50 IU/kg as primary haemostatic resuscitation in major haemorrhage before any blood
options.rationale: Prothrombinex is used for warfarin reversal or significant coagulopathy with factor deficiency, not as primary haemostatic resuscitation in undifferentiated trauma. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Massive transfusion is the transfusion of large volumes of blood products typically defined as 10 or more units of red cells in 24 hours, 4 or more units in 1 hour with ongoing need, or replacement of more than half the blood volume in 4 hours. Indications include uncontrolled haemorrhage with haemodynamic compromise (shock index over 1, lactate over 4 mmol/L, ongoing visible blood loss). Balanced product ratios (close to 1:1:1 red cells:plasma:platelets) reduce mortality compared to red cells alone. Tranexamic acid 1 g IV is given within 3 hours of injury, followed by 1 g over 8 hours. Calcium gluconate prevents citrate-induced hypocalcaemia after multiple units. Avoid hypothermia, acidosis, and hypocalcaemia (the lethal triad with coagulopathy).
key_points: Activation indicators: shock index over 1, lactate over 4 mmol/L, ongoing haemorrhage
Balanced ratios 1:1:1 reduce mortality
Permissive hypotension (SBP around 90 mmHg) until haemorrhage controlled
Calcium gluconate prevents citrate-induced hypocalcaemia
explanation.pearls: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV given within 3 hours of trauma onset followed by 1 g over 8 hours reduces all-cause mortality; later than 3 hours it may increase mortality and is not given.

sources.label: National Blood Authority Massive Transfusion Module

stem:
A 38-year-old man is brought to your emergency department with 12 hours of severe headache, fever, neck stiffness, and photophobia. His partner says 'he didn't go to work, then this evening he became drowsy and confused'. He has no immunosuppressive condition, no recent overseas travel, and no head injury. He had received the meningococcal ACWY and B vaccines in adolescence.

On examination he is drowsy (GCS 13), febrile, with marked neck stiffness, Kernig sign positive on the right, and Brudzinski sign positive. There is no focal neurological deficit, no papilloedema, no cranial nerve abnormality, and no rash. Fundoscopy is clear. Bedside lactate 2.6 mmol/L (under 2.0); fingerprick glucose 4.8 mmol/L. Blood cultures are taken now and an immediate CT head (because of the altered conscious state) is being arranged with the radiologist; lumbar puncture will follow if no contraindication. He has had no antibiotics yet.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 116 regular, BP 122/74 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 39.4 °C, GCS 13
data_table.Examination: Drowsy GCS 13, neck stiffness, Kernig positive right, Brudzinski positive, no rash, no focal deficit, fundoscopy normal
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 4.8 mmol/L, lactate 2.6 mmol/L; blood cultures taken; CT head being arranged; LP planned post-CT

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Wait for the CT head and lumbar puncture results to return before commencing any antimicrobial or steroid therapy
options.rationale: Antibiotic delay in bacterial meningitis raises mortality; empirical therapy starts immediately after blood cultures and before imaging if any delay is anticipated. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Administer intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g now and defer dexamethasone until pneumococcal infection is confirmed
options.rationale: Dexamethasone 10 mg IV is given immediately before or with the first dose of antibiotic in adult community-acquired bacterial meningitis (most benefit demonstrated in pneumococcal disease); deferring loses the chance for adjunctive benefit. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence intravenous vancomycin alone empirically for ceftriaxone-resistant pneumococcal coverage immediately
options.rationale: Vancomycin is added to ceftriaxone in areas of higher penicillin/ceftriaxone-resistant pneumococcus prevalence, not as monotherapy; the combination ensures Gram-negative and resistant pneumococcal cover. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Administer IV dexamethasone 10 mg followed immediately by IV ceftriaxone 2 g (plus IV benzylpenicillin if listeria risk)
options.rationale: Adult community-acquired bacterial meningitis empirical therapy in Australia is IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly; benzylpenicillin 2.4 g 4-hourly is added when listeria is a consideration (age over 50, immunocompromise, pregnancy). Dexamethasone 10 mg IV 6-hourly is given immediately before or with the first antibiotic dose to reduce mortality and hearing loss, particularly for pneumococcal disease. Public health notification is mandatory. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; CDNA Meningococcal Disease)

options.text: Refer to neurology for outpatient lumbar puncture and viral PCR before any antibiotic decision today
options.rationale: Outpatient management is unsafe in suspected bacterial meningitis; inpatient empirical therapy is mandatory. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Adult community-acquired bacterial meningitis is empirically treated with IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly, with benzylpenicillin or amoxicillin 2 g 4-hourly added when listeria is a consideration (age over 50, immunocompromise, pregnancy). Vancomycin is added in regions with high penicillin/ceftriaxone-resistant pneumococcal prevalence. Dexamethasone 10 mg IV 6-hourly is given immediately before or with the first antibiotic dose and continued for 4 days; greatest benefit is in pneumococcal disease, reducing mortality and hearing loss. CT head is performed before lumbar puncture only when clinical features suggest raised intracranial pressure risk (altered consciousness, focal deficit, papilloedema, seizure in the past week, immunocompromise), and never delay antibiotics for imaging. Public health notification, contact tracing, and chemoprophylaxis (ciprofloxacin or rifampicin) for close contacts of meningococcal cases are mandatory.
key_points: Empirical: IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly +/- benzylpenicillin for listeria
Dexamethasone 10 mg IV immediately before or with first antibiotic
CT before LP only with high-risk features; do not delay antibiotics
Notifiable disease - public health, contacts, chemoprophylaxis
explanation.pearls: Dexamethasone benefit is greatest if given before or with the first antibiotic dose; once antibiotics have been running for hours, the survival and hearing-loss benefit of dexamethasone is no longer demonstrable and it is not started late.

stem:
A 72-year-old man is brought to your emergency department by ambulance 90 minutes after sudden onset right-sided weakness and expressive aphasia. His wife says 'he was trying to ask for tea and the words came out scrambled, his right arm went limp and his face drooped'. The last known well time is documented at 90 minutes ago. He has type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 6.8%), hypertension on perindopril 8 mg, and atrial fibrillation on apixaban 5 mg twice daily; the last apixaban dose was at 6 am yesterday (32 hours ago). He has not had recent surgery or bleeding events.

On examination he has dense right-sided weakness (NIHSS 14), right facial droop, expressive aphasia, and right homonymous hemianopia. There is no neck stiffness and no other focal neurological deficit. A non-contrast CT head shows no haemorrhage and a hyperdense left MCA sign without established infarct; CT angiography demonstrates a left M1 MCA occlusion. Bloods show platelets 222 x10^9/L, INR 1.0, creatinine 88 micromol/L, glucose 8.4 mmol/L, anti-Xa level low (consistent with last apixaban dose 32 hours ago). His current blood pressure is 198/108 mmHg confirmed on two readings 10 minutes apart.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 92 irregularly irregular, BP 198/108 mmHg confirmed on two readings, RR 18, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: NIHSS 14, dense right hemiparesis, right facial droop, expressive aphasia, right homonymous hemianopia
data_table.Investigations: Non-contrast CT head no haemorrhage with hyperdense left MCA sign; CTA left M1 MCA occlusion; platelets 222, INR 1.0, glucose 8.4 mmol/L, anti-Xa low
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 8 mg daily; apixaban 5 mg BD (last dose 32 hours ago); metformin 1 g BD

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Proceed directly to intravenous alteplase at current blood pressure without any antihypertensive intervention now
options.rationale: Blood pressure above 185/110 mmHg is a contraindication to IV alteplase; antihypertensive control to under that threshold is required first. (Source: Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines)

options.text: Administer intravenous labetalol 10 mg or IV glyceryl trinitrate aiming for blood pressure under 180/105 mmHg, then proceed with thrombolysis
options.rationale: This option is close to correct but the AU thresholds differ: BP must be under 185/110 mmHg before alteplase and is then maintained under 180/105 mmHg for 24 hours. Aiming at 180/105 mmHg as a single pre-thrombolysis target risks borderline non-compliance with the AU criterion. (Source: Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines)

options.text: Withhold alteplase entirely due to recent apixaban exposure within the prior 48 hours regardless of anti-Xa level result
options.rationale: Direct oral anticoagulant within 48 hours is a relative contraindication, but a normal anti-Xa level (or a level below the assay-specific safe-threshold) can permit thrombolysis under stroke neurology guidance, particularly when balanced against major large-vessel occlusion benefit. (Source: Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines)

options.text: Reduce blood pressure with intravenous labetalol 10 mg to a target under 185/110 mmHg, give alteplase, and refer for thrombectomy
options.rationale: AU stroke pathway: blood pressure must be under 185/110 mmHg before IV alteplase and maintained under 180/105 mmHg for 24 hours after. IV labetalol 10-20 mg over 1-2 minutes (repeated as needed) or IV glyceryl trinitrate is the AU choice. With a confirmed large vessel occlusion (left M1 MCA) and a NIHSS over 6, endovascular thrombectomy referral is indicated alongside alteplase within the time window. The low anti-Xa level removes the apixaban barrier in this case. (Source: Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines; AHA Stroke Guideline)

options.text: Commence intravenous heparin infusion at full anticoagulation dose for atrial fibrillation-related ischaemic stroke
options.rationale: Full-dose heparin acutely after stroke increases haemorrhagic transformation; secondary prevention with oral anticoagulation is deferred typically 4-14 days depending on stroke size. (Source: Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Intravenous alteplase is offered within 4.5 hours of stroke onset (and selected patients up to 9 hours by perfusion imaging) with strict eligibility. BP must be under 185/110 mmHg before treatment and under 180/105 mmHg in the 24 hours after. AU first-line antihypertensive for pre-thrombolysis BP control is IV labetalol 10-20 mg over 1-2 minutes (with repeated doses or infusion) or IV glyceryl trinitrate. Direct oral anticoagulant within 48 hours is a relative contraindication. Endovascular thrombectomy is offered for confirmed large vessel occlusion (anterior circulation up to 24 hours by perfusion imaging) and reduces disability when added to alteplase. Tenecteplase is increasingly used in Australia and may be preferred where available.
key_points: BP under 185/110 mmHg before alteplase; under 180/105 mmHg for 24 hours after
Labetalol IV 10-20 mg over 1-2 min is AU first-line
DOAC within 48 hours: anti-Xa or thrombin level guides decision
Large vessel occlusion: thrombectomy referral alongside alteplase
explanation.pearls: Tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg as a single bolus is non-inferior to standard alteplase 0.9 mg/kg infusion in selected stroke patients in the AU pathway and is increasingly the preferred fibrinolytic where available.

stem:
A 52-year-old man is referred to your endocrinology clinic after investigation for resistant hypertension with paroxysms of headache, palpitations, and sweating. He says 'my blood pressure shoots up out of nowhere, my heart pounds, and I drench my shirt'. Twenty-four hour urinary fractionated metanephrines are 4 times the upper limit and plasma metanephrines are confirmatory. Computed tomography of the abdomen with contrast shows a 4.5 cm heterogeneous right adrenal mass with high attenuation on the non-contrast images and avid contrast enhancement. He has no family history but genetic testing is pending. Today he attends for management planning. He is otherwise well, with no chest pain, no postural symptoms, and no ECG abnormalities at rest.

On examination his BP at rest is 168/104 mmHg and rises to 192/118 mmHg when he stands suddenly. Pulse is 88. There is no cardiac murmur and no carotid bruit. Fundoscopy shows grade 2 hypertensive changes. Bloods show normal electrolytes, normal creatinine, glucose 5.6 mmol/L, normal calcium. He is currently on amlodipine 10 mg daily only. He asks about preparation for the planned right adrenalectomy in 2 weeks.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88 regular, BP 168/104 mmHg supine and 192/118 mmHg on rapid stand, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Grade 2 hypertensive retinopathy, no cardiac murmur, no postural drop, no acute neurological signs
data_table.Investigations: Plasma metanephrines elevated; 24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines 4 x ULN; CT adrenal 4.5 cm right mass with high non-contrast attenuation and avid enhancement; genetic testing pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Commence atenolol 50 mg orally daily as preoperative blockade for the planned right adrenalectomy procedure
options.rationale: Beta-blocker monotherapy in phaeochromocytoma is dangerous because unopposed alpha-adrenergic stimulation provokes severe vasoconstriction and hypertensive crisis; alpha-blockade is initiated first. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline; AMH)

options.text: Continue amlodipine 10 mg as monotherapy for blood pressure control and proceed with right adrenalectomy in two weeks as planned
options.rationale: Calcium channel blocker monotherapy is insufficient; alpha-blockade is mandatory preoperatively to control hypertensive crises and prepare for intraoperative catecholamine surge. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline)

options.text: Commence phenoxybenzamine 10 mg orally twice daily, titrate to symptom and BP control, then add beta blocker for tachycardia
options.rationale: This sequence is correct in principle - alpha first, then beta if reflex tachycardia emerges - but the dose initiation, salt/fluid liberalisation, and orthostatic monitoring framework are best captured by the more complete option D. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline)

options.text: Phenoxybenzamine titrated to mild postural drop and nasal stuffiness, salt and fluid liberalisation, then a beta blocker after alpha blockade
options.rationale: Phaeochromocytoma preoperative blockade in Australia uses an alpha blocker first (phenoxybenzamine 10 mg BD titrated up by 10-20 mg every few days until BP targets are met with mild postural drop and nasal stuffiness). Salt and fluid liberalisation expands the contracted intravascular volume. A beta blocker (atenolol or metoprolol) is added 2-3 days before surgery only after alpha-blockade is established, to address reflex tachycardia. Calcium channel blocker is an adjunct. Surgery is typically deferred 10-14 days for blockade. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline; AMH)

options.text: Administer intravenous magnesium sulfate infusion preoperatively as the sole pharmacological preparation needed
options.rationale: Magnesium sulfate is an intraoperative adjunct for catecholamine surge but is not the preoperative blockade strategy; alpha-blockade is the AU standard. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline)

explanation.summary: Phaeochromocytoma is a catecholamine-secreting tumour of the adrenal medulla (or chromaffin tissue elsewhere - paraganglioma). Hallmark triad: episodic headache, palpitations, sweating. Diagnosis is by plasma metanephrines (most sensitive) or 24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines, with localisation by CT or MRI. Hereditary syndromes (MEN2A and 2B, von Hippel-Lindau, NF1, SDHx mutations) drive 30-40% of cases and routine genetic testing is offered. Preoperative blockade prevents intraoperative hypertensive crisis: alpha-blocker first (phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin) titrated to mild postural hypotension and nasal congestion with salt and fluid liberalisation, then beta-blocker added only after alpha-blockade for reflex tachycardia, then surgery 10-14 days later. Acute hypertensive crisis intraoperatively is treated with IV phentolamine, sodium nitroprusside, or magnesium sulfate.
key_points: Alpha first, beta second: never beta-blocker alone
Salt and fluid liberalisation to expand contracted volume
Surgery 10-14 days after adequate blockade
Hereditary testing for all confirmed phaeochromocytoma
explanation.pearls: Doxazosin 2 mg orally daily, titrated up to 16 mg, is an alternative to phenoxybenzamine, with a shorter half-life that allows easier postoperative blood pressure management at the cost of less intraoperative haemodynamic stability.
